lines: If my shirt were bloody, then to shift it. Have I
hurt him?
fullText: If my shirt were bloody, then to shift it. Have I hurt him?

lines: The villain would not stand me.
fullText: The villain would not stand me.

lines: I would they had not come between us.
fullText: I would they had not come between us.

lines: And that she should love this fellow and
refuse me!
fullText: And that she should love this fellow and refuse me!

lines: Come, I'll to my chamber. Would there had
been some hurt done!
fullText: Come, I'll to my chamber. Would there had been some hurt done!

lines: You'll go with us?
fullText: You'll go with us?

lines: Nay, come, let's go together.
fullText: Nay, come, let's go together.

lines: Was there ever man had such luck? When I
kissed the jack, upon an upcast to be hit away? I
had a hundred pound on 't. And then a whoreson
jackanapes must take me up for swearing, as if I
borrowed mine oaths of him and might not spend
them at my pleasure.
fullText: Was there ever man had such luck? When I kissed the jack, upon an upcast to be hit away? I had a hundred pound on 't. And then a whoreson jackanapes must take me up for swearing, as if I borrowed mine oaths of him and might not spend them at my pleasure.

lines: When a gentleman is disposed to swear, it is
not for any standers-by to curtail his oaths, ha?
fullText: When a gentleman is disposed to swear, it is not for any standers-by to curtail his oaths, ha?

lines: Whoreson dog! I gave him satisfaction. Would
he had been one of my rank.
fullText: Whoreson dog! I gave him satisfaction. Would he had been one of my rank.

lines: I am not vexed more at anything in th' Earth.
A pox on 't! I had rather not be so noble as I am.
They dare not fight with me because of the Queen
my mother. Every jack-slave hath his bellyful of
fighting, and I must go up and down like a cock
that nobody can match.
fullText: I am not vexed more at anything in th' Earth. A pox on 't! I had rather not be so noble as I am. They dare not fight with me because of the Queen my mother. Every jack-slave hath his bellyful of fighting, and I must go up and down like a cock that nobody can match.

lines: Sayest thou?
fullText: Sayest thou?

lines: No, I know that, but it is fit I should commit
offense to my inferiors.
fullText: No, I know that, but it is fit I should commit offense to my inferiors.

lines: Why, so I say.
fullText: Why, so I say.

lines: A stranger, and I not know on 't?
fullText: A stranger, and I not know on 't?

lines: Leonatus? A banished rascal; and he's another,
whatsoever he be. Who told you of this stranger?
fullText: Leonatus? A banished rascal; and he's another, whatsoever he be. Who told you of this stranger?

lines: Is it fit I went to look upon him? Is there no
derogation in 't?
fullText: Is it fit I went to look upon him? Is there no derogation in 't?

lines: Not easily, I think.
fullText: Not easily, I think.

lines: Come, I'll go see this Italian. What I have lost
today at bowls I'll win tonight of him. Come, go.
fullText: Come, I'll go see this Italian. What I have lost today at bowls I'll win tonight of him. Come, go.

lines: It would make any man cold to lose.
fullText: It would make any man cold to lose.

lines: Winning will put any man into courage. If I
could get this foolish Imogen, I should have gold
enough. It's almost morning, is 't not?
fullText: Winning will put any man into courage. If I could get this foolish Imogen, I should have gold enough. It's almost morning, is 't not?

lines: I would this music would come. I am advised
to give her music a-mornings; they say it will
penetrate.
fullText: I would this music would come. I am advised to give her music a-mornings; they say it will penetrate.

lines: So, get you gone. If this penetrate, I will
consider your music the better. If it do not, it is a
vice in her ears which horsehairs and calves'
guts, nor the voice of unpaved eunuch to boot, can
never amend.
fullText: So, get you gone. If this penetrate, I will consider your music the better. If it do not, it is a vice in her ears which horsehairs and calves' guts, nor the voice of unpaved eunuch to boot, can never amend.

lines: I am glad I was up so late, for that's the reason
I was up so early. He cannot choose but take this
service I have done fatherly.--Good morrow to
your Majesty and to my gracious mother.
fullText: I am glad I was up so late, for that's the reason I was up so early. He cannot choose but take this service I have done fatherly.--Good morrow to your Majesty and to my gracious mother.

lines: I have assailed her with musics, but she
vouchsafes no notice.
fullText: I have assailed her with musics, but she vouchsafes no notice.

lines: Senseless? Not so.
fullText: Senseless? Not so.

lines: If she be up, I'll speak with her; if not,
Let her lie still and dream. By your
leave, ho!--
I know her women are about her. What
If I do line one of their hands? 'Tis gold
Which buys admittance--oft it doth--yea, and makes
Diana's rangers false themselves, yield up
Their deer to th' stand o' th' stealer; and 'tis gold
Which makes the true man killed and saves the thief,
Nay, sometime hangs both thief and true man. What
Can it not do and undo? I will make
One of her women lawyer to me, for
I yet not understand the case myself.
By your leave.
fullText: If she be up, I'll speak with her; if not, Let her lie still and dream. By your leave, ho!-- I know her women are about her. What If I do line one of their hands? 'Tis gold Which buys admittance--oft it doth--yea, and makes Diana's rangers false themselves, yield up Their deer to th' stand o' th' stealer; and 'tis gold Which makes the true man killed and saves the thief, Nay, sometime hangs both thief and true man. What Can it not do and undo? I will make One of her women lawyer to me, for I yet not understand the case myself. By your leave.

lines: A gentleman.
fullText: A gentleman.

lines: Yes, and a gentlewoman's son.
fullText: Yes, and a gentlewoman's son.

lines: Your lady's person. Is she ready?
fullText: Your lady's person. Is she ready?

lines: There is gold for you.
Sell me your good report.
fullText: There is gold for you. Sell me your good report.

lines: Good morrow, fairest sister. Your sweet hand.
fullText: Good morrow, fairest sister. Your sweet hand.

lines: Still I swear I love you.
fullText: Still I swear I love you.

lines: This is no answer.
fullText: This is no answer.

lines: To leave you in your madness 'twere my sin.
I will not.
fullText: To leave you in your madness 'twere my sin. I will not.

lines: Do you call me fool?
fullText: Do you call me fool?

lines: You sin against
Obedience, which you owe your father. For
The contract you pretend with that base wretch--
One bred of alms and fostered with cold dishes,
With scraps o' th' court--it is no contract, none;
And though it be allowed in meaner parties--
Yet who than he more mean?--to knit their souls,
On whom there is no more dependency
But brats and beggary, in self-figured knot;
Yet you are curbed from that enlargement by
The consequence o' th' crown, and must not foil
The precious note of it with a base slave,
A hilding for a livery, a squire's cloth,
A pantler--not so eminent.
fullText: You sin against Obedience, which you owe your father. For The contract you pretend with that base wretch-- One bred of alms and fostered with cold dishes, With scraps o' th' court--it is no contract, none; And though it be allowed in meaner parties-- Yet who than he more mean?--to knit their souls, On whom there is no more dependency But brats and beggary, in self-figured knot; Yet you are curbed from that enlargement by The consequence o' th' crown, and must not foil The precious note of it with a base slave, A hilding for a livery, a squire's cloth, A pantler--not so eminent.

lines: The south fog rot him!
fullText: The south fog rot him!

lines: "His garment"? Now the devil--
fullText: "His garment"? Now the devil--

lines: "His garment"?
fullText: "His garment"?

lines: You have abused me.
"His meanest garment"?
fullText: You have abused me. "His meanest garment"?

lines: I will inform your father.
fullText: I will inform your father.

lines: I'll be revenged! "His mean'st garment"? Well.
fullText: I'll be revenged! "His mean'st garment"? Well.

lines: There be many Caesars
Ere such another Julius. Britain's a world
By itself, and we will nothing pay
For wearing our own noses.
fullText: There be many Caesars Ere such another Julius. Britain's a world By itself, and we will nothing pay For wearing our own noses.

lines: Come, there's no more tribute to be paid. Our
kingdom is stronger than it was at that time, and,
as I said, there is no more such Caesars. Other of
them may have crooked noses, but to owe such
straight arms, none.
fullText: Come, there's no more tribute to be paid. Our kingdom is stronger than it was at that time, and, as I said, there is no more such Caesars. Other of them may have crooked noses, but to owe such straight arms, none.

lines: We have yet many among us can grip as hard
as Cassibelan. I do not say I am one, but I have a
hand. Why tribute? Why should we pay tribute? If
Caesar can hide the sun from us with a blanket or
put the moon in his pocket, we will pay him tribute
for light; else, sir, no more tribute, pray you now.
fullText: We have yet many among us can grip as hard as Cassibelan. I do not say I am one, but I have a hand. Why tribute? Why should we pay tribute? If Caesar can hide the sun from us with a blanket or put the moon in his pocket, we will pay him tribute for light; else, sir, no more tribute, pray you now.

lines: His Majesty bids you welcome. Make pastime
with us a day or two, or longer. If you seek us afterwards
in other terms, you shall find us in our saltwater
girdle; if you beat us out of it, it is yours. If
you fall in the adventure, our crows shall fare the
better for you, and there's an end.
fullText: His Majesty bids you welcome. Make pastime with us a day or two, or longer. If you seek us afterwards in other terms, you shall find us in our saltwater girdle; if you beat us out of it, it is yours. If you fall in the adventure, our crows shall fare the better for you, and there's an end.

lines: Receive it friendly, but from this time forth
I wear it as your enemy.
fullText: Receive it friendly, but from this time forth I wear it as your enemy.

lines: 'Tis all the better.
Your valiant Britons have their wishes in it.
fullText: 'Tis all the better. Your valiant Britons have their wishes in it.

lines: That man of hers, Pisanio, her old servant
I have not seen these two days.
fullText: That man of hers, Pisanio, her old servant I have not seen these two days.

lines: 'Tis certain she is fled.
Go in and cheer the King. He rages; none
Dare come about him.
fullText: 'Tis certain she is fled. Go in and cheer the King. He rages; none Dare come about him.

lines: I love and hate her, for she's fair and royal,
And that she hath all courtly parts more exquisite
Than lady, ladies, woman. From every one
The best she hath, and she, of all compounded,
Outsells them all. I love her therefore, but
Disdaining me and throwing favors on
The low Posthumus slanders so her judgment
That what's else rare is choked. And in that point
I will conclude to hate her, nay, indeed,
To be revenged upon her. For, when fools
Shall--
fullText: I love and hate her, for she's fair and royal, And that she hath all courtly parts more exquisite Than lady, ladies, woman. From every one The best she hath, and she, of all compounded, Outsells them all. I love her therefore, but Disdaining me and throwing favors on The low Posthumus slanders so her judgment That what's else rare is choked. And in that point I will conclude to hate her, nay, indeed, To be revenged upon her. For, when fools Shall--

lines: Where is thy lady? Or, by Jupiter--
I will not ask again. Close villain,
I'll have this secret from thy heart or rip
Thy heart to find it. Is she with Posthumus,
From whose so many weights of baseness cannot
A dram of worth be drawn?
fullText: Where is thy lady? Or, by Jupiter-- I will not ask again. Close villain, I'll have this secret from thy heart or rip Thy heart to find it. Is she with Posthumus, From whose so many weights of baseness cannot A dram of worth be drawn?

lines: Where is she, sir? Come nearer.
No farther halting. Satisfy me home
What is become of her.
fullText: Where is she, sir? Come nearer. No farther halting. Satisfy me home What is become of her.

lines: All-worthy villain!
Discover where thy mistress is at once,
At the next word. No more of "worthy lord"!
Speak, or thy silence on the instant is
Thy condemnation and thy death.
fullText: All-worthy villain! Discover where thy mistress is at once, At the next word. No more of "worthy lord"! Speak, or thy silence on the instant is Thy condemnation and thy death.

lines: Let's see 't. I will pursue her
Even to Augustus' throne.
fullText: Let's see 't. I will pursue her Even to Augustus' throne.

lines: Humh!
fullText: Humh!

lines: Sirrah, is this letter true?
fullText: Sirrah, is this letter true?

lines: It is Posthumus' hand, I know 't. Sirrah, if
thou wouldst not be a villain, but do me true service,
undergo those employments wherein I should
have cause to use thee with a serious industry--
that is, what villainy soe'er I bid thee do to perform
it directly and truly--I would think thee an honest
man. Thou shouldst neither want my means for thy
relief nor my voice for thy preferment.
fullText: It is Posthumus' hand, I know 't. Sirrah, if thou wouldst not be a villain, but do me true service, undergo those employments wherein I should have cause to use thee with a serious industry-- that is, what villainy soe'er I bid thee do to perform it directly and truly--I would think thee an honest man. Thou shouldst neither want my means for thy relief nor my voice for thy preferment.

lines: Wilt thou serve me? For since patiently and
constantly thou hast stuck to the bare fortune of
that beggar Posthumus, thou canst not in the
course of gratitude but be a diligent follower of
mine. Wilt thou serve me?
fullText: Wilt thou serve me? For since patiently and constantly thou hast stuck to the bare fortune of that beggar Posthumus, thou canst not in the course of gratitude but be a diligent follower of mine. Wilt thou serve me?

lines: Give me thy hand. Here's my purse. [Gives
him money.] Hast any of thy late master's garments
in thy possession?
fullText: Give me thy hand. Here's my purse. [Gives him money.] Hast any of thy late master's garments in thy possession?

lines: The first service thou dost me, fetch that suit
hither. Let it be thy first service. Go.
fullText: The first service thou dost me, fetch that suit hither. Let it be thy first service. Go.

lines: Meet thee at Milford Haven!--I forgot to ask
him one thing; I'll remember 't anon. Even there,
thou villain Posthumus, will I kill thee. I would
these garments were come. She said upon a time--
the bitterness of it I now belch from my heart--
that she held the very garment of Posthumus in
more respect than my noble and natural person,
together with the adornment of my qualities. With
that suit upon my back will I ravish her. First, kill
him, and in her eyes. There shall she see my valor,
which will then be a torment to her contempt.
He on the ground, my speech of insultment
ended on his dead body, and when my lust hath
dined--which, as I say, to vex her I will execute
in the clothes that she so praised--to the court
I'll knock her back, foot her home again. She hath
despised me rejoicingly, and I'll be merry in my
revenge.
fullText: Meet thee at Milford Haven!--I forgot to ask him one thing; I'll remember 't anon. Even there, thou villain Posthumus, will I kill thee. I would these garments were come. She said upon a time-- the bitterness of it I now belch from my heart-- that she held the very garment of Posthumus in more respect than my noble and natural person, together with the adornment of my qualities. With that suit upon my back will I ravish her. First, kill him, and in her eyes. There shall she see my valor, which will then be a torment to her contempt. He on the ground, my speech of insultment ended on his dead body, and when my lust hath dined--which, as I say, to vex her I will execute in the clothes that she so praised--to the court I'll knock her back, foot her home again. She hath despised me rejoicingly, and I'll be merry in my revenge.

lines: How long is 't since she went to Milford Haven?
fullText: How long is 't since she went to Milford Haven?

lines: Bring this apparel to my chamber; that is the
second thing that I have commanded thee. The
third is that thou wilt be a voluntary mute to my
design. Be but duteous, and true preferment shall
tender itself to thee. My revenge is now at Milford.
Would I had wings to follow it! Come, and be true.
fullText: Bring this apparel to my chamber; that is the second thing that I have commanded thee. The third is that thou wilt be a voluntary mute to my design. Be but duteous, and true preferment shall tender itself to thee. My revenge is now at Milford. Would I had wings to follow it! Come, and be true.

lines: I am near to th' place where they should meet,
if Pisanio have mapped it truly. How fit his garments
serve me! Why should his mistress, who
was made by him that made the tailor, not be fit
too? The rather, saving reverence of the word, for
'tis said a woman's fitness comes by fits. Therein I
must play the workman. I dare speak it to myself,
for it is not vainglory for a man and his glass to
confer in his own chamber. I mean, the lines of my
body are as well drawn as his, no less young, more
strong; not beneath him in fortunes, beyond him
in the advantage of the time, above him in birth,
alike conversant in general services, and more remarkable
in single oppositions. Yet this imperceiverant
thing loves him in my despite. What
mortality is! Posthumus, thy head, which now is
growing upon thy shoulders, shall within this hour
be off, thy mistress enforced, thy garments cut to
pieces before thy face; and all this done, spurn her
home to her father, who may haply be a little angry
or my so rough usage. But my mother, having
power of his testiness, shall turn all into my commendations.
My horse is tied up safe. Out, sword,
and to a sore purpose. Fortune, put them into my
hand! This is the very description of their meeting
place, and the fellow dares not deceive me.
fullText: I am near to th' place where they should meet, if Pisanio have mapped it truly. How fit his garments serve me! Why should his mistress, who was made by him that made the tailor, not be fit too? The rather, saving reverence of the word, for 'tis said a woman's fitness comes by fits. Therein I must play the workman. I dare speak it to myself, for it is not vainglory for a man and his glass to confer in his own chamber. I mean, the lines of my body are as well drawn as his, no less young, more strong; not beneath him in fortunes, beyond him in the advantage of the time, above him in birth, alike conversant in general services, and more remarkable in single oppositions. Yet this imperceiverant thing loves him in my despite. What mortality is! Posthumus, thy head, which now is growing upon thy shoulders, shall within this hour be off, thy mistress enforced, thy garments cut to pieces before thy face; and all this done, spurn her home to her father, who may haply be a little angry or my so rough usage. But my mother, having power of his testiness, shall turn all into my commendations. My horse is tied up safe. Out, sword, and to a sore purpose. Fortune, put them into my hand! This is the very description of their meeting place, and the fellow dares not deceive me.

lines: I cannot find those runagates. That villain
Hath mocked me. I am faint.
fullText: I cannot find those runagates. That villain Hath mocked me. I am faint.

lines: Soft, what are you
That fly me thus? Some villain mountaineers?
I have heard of such.--What slave art thou?
fullText: Soft, what are you That fly me thus? Some villain mountaineers? I have heard of such.--What slave art thou?

lines: Thou art a robber,
A lawbreaker, a villain. Yield thee, thief.
fullText: Thou art a robber, A lawbreaker, a villain. Yield thee, thief.

lines: Thou villain base,
Know'st me not by my clothes?
fullText: Thou villain base, Know'st me not by my clothes?

lines: Thou precious varlet,
My tailor made them not.
fullText: Thou precious varlet, My tailor made them not.

lines: Thou injurious thief,
Hear but my name, and tremble.
fullText: Thou injurious thief, Hear but my name, and tremble.

lines: Cloten, thou villain.
fullText: Cloten, thou villain.

lines: To thy further fear,
Nay, to thy mere confusion, thou shalt know
I am son to th' Queen.
fullText: To thy further fear, Nay, to thy mere confusion, thou shalt know I am son to th' Queen.

lines: Art not afeard?
fullText: Art not afeard?

lines: Die the death!
When I have slain thee with my proper hand,
I'll follow those that even now fled hence
And on the gates of Lud's Town set your heads.
Yield, rustic mountaineer!
fullText: Die the death! When I have slain thee with my proper hand, I'll follow those that even now fled hence And on the gates of Lud's Town set your heads. Yield, rustic mountaineer!
